text: Perish, because she hath stirred up her God to bitterness. It is not a curse or imprecation, but a prophecy of what should come to pass (Challoner) to Israel, in Assyria. Many such expressions occur, Psalm 68:25., etc. (St. Jerome)<hr>Sometimes they are the effects of zeal, conformable to divine justice, Psalm 140:6. (Worthington)<hr>Hebrew, "Samaria has sinned, or shall perish." (Calmet)<hr>Bitterness. Septuagint, "she hath resisted her God." (Haydock)

Words. In captivity, legal victims cannot be offered. (Calmet)<hr>But a contrite heart is always acceptable, Psalm L.<hr>Good. While engaged in sin, (Haydock) "we can offer thee nothing good."<hr>Calves: victims of praise. (St. Jerome)<hr>Hebrew parim. Septuagint omit m, (Haydock) and render fruit. They are followed by the Arabic and Syriac as well as by the apostle, Hebrews 13:15. (Calmet)<hr>We will offer what victims we please. (Estius) (Psalms 69:23., and 65:13.)

Gods. The Assyrians, instead of protecting, oppress us; while Egypt, famous for horses, sits unconcerned. (Calmet)<hr>But the source of all our evils are the idols, which we will follow no more.<hr>In thee: adheres to the true faith in practice. (Haydock)<hr>Israel was like an orphan during the captivity, Lamentations 1:1. (Calmet)

Breaches, when Israel shall be converted, as some were to Christ, and many will be at the end of the world. (Worthington)<hr>Hebrew, "their return." Septuagint, "dwellings." They shall be purified.<hr>Freely. I have been forced to chastise, My heart dilates. (Calmet)<hr>Septuagint, "I will love them manifestly." Syriac, "accept their free offerings." (Haydock)
Dew. Israel has been like a plant dried up, Osee 13:15.<hr>Libanus. The cedars were tall and bulky, being well rooted.
Glory. Septuagint, "he shall be as fruitful as the olive-tree."<hr>Libanus, or incense. (Calmet)<hr>The term has both meanings. (Haydock)

His. This may refer to the tree, or to God. The captives shall return, and be happy. But in a more sublime sense it refers to the nations which shall embrace the gospel.<hr>Libanus, or fragrant. Such wine was esteemed in which certain odoriferous herbs were infused, Canticle of Canticles 7:2. (Calmet)<hr>Libanus was also famous for generous wines. (Siconita 11.)
Idols? or God will no more reproach them, as their conversion is sincere.<hr>Make. Hebrew, "be to him like," etc. (Calmet)

Wise. This denotes the obscurity of the prophecy. (Theodoret)<hr>No human wit can explain the prophets: yet the just shall understand as much as shall be necessary. (St. Jerome; St. Augustine, City of God 18:28.) (Worthington)<hr>Only few will make good use of these admonitions, and share in the promises. (Calmet)
